name: DEC PDP-1 — MACRO assembly instruction set
source: Based on the PDP-1 Handbook (F-15D, DEC, Maynard 1963) with PDP-1D (1964) updates.
ref: Norbert Landsteiner, “Inside Spacewar! — PDP-1 Instructions”, masswerk.at
note: Lookups match the leading mnemonic of a token (case-insensitive). Each entry gives the full name, parameters, the effect (with octal opcode), and a small example. Octal opcodes are 2 digits for memory-reference instructions, 6 for the micro-coded operate/skip/shift/IOT groups.

lac.name: Load Accumulator
lac.description: AC ← C(Y). Copy the contents of memory register Y into the accumulator. The fundamental load: arithmetic and logic happen in the AC, so almost every computation begins here.

lio.name: Load In-Out register
lio.description: IO ← C(Y). Copy memory word Y into the IO register (the 18-bit register used for I/O, display coordinates, and the low half of double-length arithmetic).

dac.name: Deposit Accumulator
dac.description: C(Y) ← AC. Store the accumulator into memory register Y. The companion of lac: load, compute, deposit.
dac.example: dac ssbx   / write the new x back

dap.name: Deposit Address Part
dap.description: Store AC bits 6–17 (the address field) into Y, leaving Y's opcode bits 0–5 intact. The classic self-modifying-code idiom: patch where an instruction points without rewriting the instruction.
dap.example:
lac p
     dap ptr   / patch ptr's address to p
ptr, lac .

dip.name: Deposit Instruction Part
dip.description: Store AC bits 0–5 (the opcode field) into Y, leaving Y's address bits 6–17 intact. The opcode-half twin of dap.
dip.example: dip insn  / patch only the opcode of insn

dio.name: Deposit In-Out register
dio.description: C(Y) ← IO. Store the IO register into memory register Y.
dio.example: dio temp  / save IO for later

dzm.name: Deposit Zero in Memory
dzm.description: C(Y) ← 0. Clear memory register Y to zero.

add.name: Add
add.description: AC ← AC + C(Y), ones-complement addition; sets the Overflow flip-flop on a carry out of the sign bit.
add.example:
lac x
     add y   / AC = x + y

sub.name: Subtract
sub.example:
lac x
     sub y   / AC = x - y

tad.name: Two's-complement Add
tad.description: AC ← AC + C(Y) in two's-complement (PDP-1D). Adds without the ones-complement end-around carry.

idx.name: Index
idx.description: C(Y) ← C(Y) + 1, and the result is also left in AC. Increment a memory word in place (a loop counter, a pointer).

isp.name: Index and Skip if Positive
isp.description: Increment C(Y) (result also in AC) and skip the next instruction if the result is positive (≥ 0). The standard count-up loop test.
isp.example:
loop, ...
     isp i
     jmp loop  / repeat until i goes positive

and.name: Logical AND
and.description: AC ← AC ∧ C(Y). Bit-by-bit logical AND with memory word Y. Used for masking.
and.example: and mask  / keep only the masked bits

ior.name: Inclusive OR
ior.description: AC ← AC ∨ C(Y). Bit-by-bit inclusive OR with memory word Y. Used to set bits.
ior.example: ior flag  / turn the flag bits on

xor.name: Exclusive OR
xor.description: AC ← AC ⊕ C(Y). Bit-by-bit exclusive OR with memory word Y. Used to toggle/compare bits.

xct.name: Execute
xct.description: Execute the instruction held in register Y, in place, without branching. A one-instruction interpreter: lets a register hold an instruction to be run on demand.
xct.example: xct insn  / run the instruction stored in insn

sad.name: Skip if AC and memory Differ
sad.description: Skip the next instruction if AC ≠ C(Y).

sas.name: Skip if AC and memory Same
sas.description: Skip the next instruction if AC = C(Y).

mus.name: Multiply Step
mus.description: One step of the multiply routine (base PDP-1 had no hardware multiply; a 'mul' option existed later).

mul.name: Multiply
mul.description: Hardware multiply (automatic multiply/divide option). AC,IO ← AC × C(Y).
mul.example:
lac a
     mul b   / AC,IO = a * b

dis.name: Divide Step
dis.description: One step of the divide routine.

div.name: Divide
div.description: Hardware divide (automatic option). The next instruction is skipped if there was no overflow.

jmp.name: Jump
jmp.description: PC ← Y. Take the next instruction from register Y. Unconditional branch. 'jmp .' loops on itself; 'jmp .-1' loops on the previous instruction (busy-wait).

jsp.name: Jump and Save Program counter
jsp.description: AC ← return address (the PC), then PC ← Y. The classic subroutine call; the callee returns with 'jmp' through the saved address.

jda.name: Jump and Deposit Accumulator
jda.description: Equivalent to dac Y followed by jsp Y+1: store AC at Y, then call Y+1. A subroutine call that also passes the accumulator and reserves Y as its return slot.
jda.example: jda sqrt  / sqrt of AC, returns in AC

cal.name: Call subroutine
cal.description: Equivalent to jda 100 (octal): deposit the return linkage at register 100 and jump to 101. Fixed-address subroutine linkage for systems with one common call site.

law.name: Load AC With (immediate)
law.description: AC ← N, the literal number in the address field (immediate load, opcode 70). 'law i N' (opcode 71) loads −N. No memory fetch — the constant is in the instruction.

lap.name: Load AC with Program counter
lap.description: AC ← Program Counter (an operate micro-instruction). Reads where execution currently is.

lat.name: Load AC from Test word
lat.description: AC ← the front-panel Test Word switches (an operate micro-instruction). The operator's live input to a running program.

iot.name: In-Out Transfer
iot.description: Initiate or complete a transfer with an I/O device, selected by the device bits in Y. The umbrella opcode under which all the named device mnemonics (tyo, dpy, rpa…) live. 'iot 11' reads the Spacewar! control boxes.
iot.example: iot 11  / read the MIT Spacewar! controllers

opr.name: Operate group
opr.description: The micro-coded operate group (clear, complement, halt, no-op, load PC/test, set/clear flags) selected by bits in the address field. Several can be OR-combined in one word.
opr.example: cla+cli   / clear AC and IO at once

clc.name: Clear and Complement
clc.description: Macro: cla+cma — clear the accumulator then complement it, giving all-ones (−0). Often defined at the top of a program: clc=cma+cla-opr.

cma.name: Complement Accumulator
cma.example: cma   / flip every bit of AC

hlt.name: Halt
hlt.description: Stop the processor (an operate micro-instruction). The run light goes out until the operator restarts.

nop.name: No Operation
nop.description: Do nothing for one memory cycle (an operate word with no bits set). Used for timing or as a patch placeholder.

swp.name: Swap AC and IO
swp.description: Exchange the AC and IO registers (PDP-1D).

stf.name: Set Flag
stf.description: Set program flag f (1–6). The program flags are general-purpose condition bits the program manages itself.

clf.name: Clear Flag
clf.description: Clear program flag f (1–6). 'clf 7' clears all flags.

skp.name: Skip group
skp.description: The micro-coded conditional-skip group (sza, spa, sma, szo, spi, szs, szf…). Adding the defer bit 'i' inverts the sense of the test.
skp.example: sza i   / skip if AC is NOT zero

sza.name: Skip on Zero Accumulator
sza.description: Skip the next instruction if AC = +0.

spa.name: Skip on Plus Accumulator
spa.description: Skip the next instruction if the AC sign (bit 0) is positive.

sma.name: Skip on Minus Accumulator
sma.description: Skip the next instruction if the AC sign (bit 0) is negative.

szo.name: Skip on Zero Overflow
szo.description: Skip the next instruction if the overflow flip-flop is clear, and clear it.

spi.name: Skip on Plus In-Out register
spi.description: Skip the next instruction if the IO sign bit is positive.

szs.name: Skip on Zero Sense switch
szs.description: Skip the next instruction if the selected front-panel sense switch s (1–6) is zero. Lets the operator steer a running program from the console.

szf.name: Skip on Zero Flag
szf.description: Skip the next instruction if the selected program flag f is zero.

sni.name: Skip on Non-zero IO
sni.description: Skip the next instruction if IO ≠ 0 (PDP-1D).

szi.name: Skip on Zero IO
szi.description: Skip the next instruction if IO = 0 (PDP-1D).

sft.name: Shift/rotate group
sft.description: The shift and rotate micro-instructions (ral, rar, rcl, rcr, sal, sar, scl, scr, ril, rir, sil, sir). The count is a unary mask in the address field: 1s, 2s … 9s shift by 1–9 places.

ral.name: Rotate Accumulator Left
ral.description: Circular left rotate of the AC by N places (bits shifted off the left re-enter on the right).
ral.example: ral 1s  / rotate AC left one bit

rar.name: Rotate Accumulator Right
rar.description: Circular right rotate of the AC by N places.

rcl.name: Rotate Combined Left
rcl.description: Circular left rotate of the 36-bit combined AC+IO register by N places.

rcr.name: Rotate Combined Right
rcr.description: Circular right rotate of the combined AC+IO register by N places.
rcr.example: rcr 9s  / used in Spacewar! to step the angle tables

ril.name: Rotate In-Out Left
ril.description: Circular left rotate of the IO register by N places.

rir.name: Rotate In-Out Right
rir.description: Circular right rotate of the IO register by N places.

sal.name: Shift Accumulator Left
sal.description: Arithmetic (sign-preserving) left shift of the AC by N places; zeros fill the right.

sar.name: Shift Accumulator Right
sar.description: Arithmetic right shift of the AC by N places; the sign bit propagates.

scl.name: Shift Combined Left
scl.description: Arithmetic left shift of the combined AC+IO register by N places.

scr.name: Shift Combined Right
scr.description: Arithmetic right shift of the combined AC+IO register by N places.

sil.name: Shift In-Out Left
sil.description: Arithmetic left shift of the IO register by N places.

sir.name: Shift In-Out Right
sir.description: Arithmetic right shift of the IO register by N places.

tyo.name: Type Out
tyo.description: Send the character in IO to the console typewriter (an IOT).
tyo.example:
lio ch
     tyo   / print the character

tyi.name: Type In
tyi.description: Read a typed character into IO (an IOT). In Spacewar! the keyboard test-word reading uses the in-out system.

rpa.name: Read Perforated tape, Alphanumeric
rpa.description: Read one line of paper tape in alphanumeric mode into IO (an IOT).

rpb.name: Read Perforated tape, Binary
rpb.description: Read paper tape in binary mode into IO (an IOT). How a program (Spacewar! included) was loaded.

rrb.name: Read Reader Buffer
rrb.description: Read the paper-tape reader buffer into IO (an IOT).

ppa.name: Punch Perforated tape, Alphanumeric
ppa.description: Punch the character in IO to paper tape in alphanumeric mode (an IOT).

ppb.name: Punch Perforated tape, Binary
ppb.description: Punch IO to paper tape in binary mode (an IOT).

dpy.name: Display One Point
dpy.description: Plot a single point on the Type 30 precision CRT at the coordinates held in AC (x) and IO (y); intensity is set by bits 9–11. This is the instruction that drew Spacewar! — every star, ship, and torpedo is a stream of dpy points.
dpy.example:
lac x
     lio y
     dpy   / light one pixel

dpp.name: Display One Point, Precision
dpp.description: Plot one point on the ultra-precision CRT (an IOT).

cks.name: Check Status
cks.description: Read the in-out device status flags into IO (an IOT), so the program can poll which devices are ready.

esm.name: Enter Sequence-break Mode
esm.description: Activate the sequence-break (interrupt) system, so I/O completion can break in on the running program.

lsm.name: Leave Sequence-break Mode
lsm.description: Deactivate the sequence-break (interrupt) system. Spacewar! runs with the break system off and polls instead, so it opens with lsm to take full control of timing.

cbs.name: Clear Sequence-Break system
cbs.description: Clear the whole sequence-break system (an IOT).

iot11.name: Read Spacewar! controllers
iot11.description: Read the four MIT Spacewar! control boxes into IO — the custom paddle/switch controllers built so players' hands stayed off the main console. Bits encode turn-left, turn-right, thrust, and fire for each ship.
iot11.example:
iot 11
     lio cntrl / IO ← all four players' inputs

macro.name: MACRO directive — define a macro
macro.description: Begin a macro-instruction definition. The body that follows (until 'term') is substituted wherever the macro name is used, with arguments filled in. The PDP-1 MACRO assembler (and its MIDAS successor) made Spacewar!'s dense code readable.
macro.example:
define xincr X,Y,INS
     lac Y
     INS \ssn
     dac Y
     term

define.name: MACRO/MIDAS directive — define
define.description: Define a macro: the body follows until 'term', and the named macro expands inline with its arguments substituted. The workhorse abstraction of PDP-1 assembly.

term.name: MACRO directive — terminate
term.description: End the current macro definition begun by 'define' / 'macro'.
term.example:
define x
     ...
     term   / end of macro x

repeat.name: MACRO directive — repeat
repeat.description: Repeat the following text or instruction n times at assembly time. Used for unrolling and for building tables.
repeat.example: repeat 3, A=A+A   / triple A in place

constants.name: MACRO directive — constants pool
constants.description: Emit the pool of literal constants accumulated by the assembler at this point in memory.

variables.name: MACRO directive — variables
variables.description: Reserve storage at this point for all the variables the program declared.

start.name: MACRO directive — start
start.description: Mark the program's entry point (the address execution begins at after loading).

ins.name: Macro parameter (not an instruction)
ins.params: an argument name, e.g. in xincr X,Y,INS
ins.description: INS is NOT a PDP-1 instruction — it is a macro PARAMETER, a placeholder for an instruction substituted in when the macro is called. In Spacewar!, xincr/yincr take an INS argument (typically 'add' or 'sub'): the body applies INS to the Y coordinate against \ssn (the sine of the ship's heading) and to X against \scn (the cosine), so the macro adds — or, with 'sub', subtracts — a unit velocity vector to the ship's position. One macro, reused for both directions by swapping the instruction. (Right-click 'xincr' at a call site and use Jump to definition to see the define … term block.)
ins.example:
xincr ssbx,ssby,add   / INS = add → ship moves +
xincr ssbx,ssby,sub   / INS = sub → ship moves −
